event: The Transfiguration of Christ on Mount Tabor
symbols: radiant white Christ — divine glory, the uncreated light of the Godhead made visible in the flesh
Moses on one side — the Law, the Torah, the Old Covenant that Christ fulfills
Elijah on the other side — the Prophets, prophetic tradition pointing to the Messiah
mountain setting — place of divine encounter, Sinai, Horeb, Zion
bright cloud — the Shekinah glory, the overshadowing presence of the Father
prostrate disciples — human incapacity before divine glory, holy fear and awe
white garments of Christ — heavenly purity, resurrection glory, eschatological transformation
description: A painted altarpiece panel depicting the Transfiguration. At center, Christ stands elevated, clothed in brilliant white robes, his body emanating golden-white light that breaks through dark swirling clouds. His arms are slightly extended. To his upper left, Moses is shown as a bearded elder figure holding or reading from a book, and to his upper right, Elijah appears as a bearded prophet. Below, three disciples are shown in dramatic postures of collapse and awe: one in red robes falls forward shielding his face, another in green looks up with astonishment, and the third crouches low. The landscape behind includes rocky terrain and trees. The panel is set within a painted wooden frame.
theological_description: Jan Joest's Transfiguration presents one of the most theologically dense events in the Gospels. The Synoptic accounts (Matthew 17:1-8, Mark 9:2-8, Luke 9:28-36) record the moment when Christ's divine nature blazed through his human flesh on the mountain, and Joest renders this as blinding radiance emanating from the glorified body. The Church Fathers understood this event as a revelation not of new glory acquired but of the eternal divine glory that Christ always possessed but voluntarily veiled in the Incarnation. John of Damascus wrote that Christ was transfigured not by assuming what he was not but by manifesting to his disciples what he truly was. The presence of Moses and Elijah -- the Law and the Prophets -- constitutes a visual hermeneutic of the entire Old Testament: everything in Scripture converges upon and is fulfilled in Christ. Luke alone records that they spoke of his 'exodus' to be accomplished at Jerusalem (Luke 9:31), binding the Transfiguration to the Cross. The three prostrate disciples embody the theological truth that fallen human nature cannot sustain the direct vision of God; as God told Moses, 'no one shall see me and live' (Exodus 33:20). Yet here, by grace, the veil is briefly lifted. The Eastern Fathers, especially Gregory Palamas, developed an entire theology of the uncreated light from this event -- the Tabor light is not created illumination but the very energies of God. The Transfiguration also functions as an eschatological preview: Christ's glorified body anticipates the resurrection and the final transformation of all creation. Peter's confused offer to build three booths (Mark 9:5) connects to the Feast of Tabernacles and the eschatological ingathering, while the Father's voice from the cloud -- 'This is my beloved Son; listen to him' -- recapitulates the baptismal declaration and the Deuteronomic promise of the Prophet like Moses (Deuteronomy 18:15).

scripture_refs.reason: The direct biblical narrative of the Transfiguration depicted in this painting
Mark's account of the Transfiguration with the detail of garments becoming dazzling white
Luke's account uniquely recording that Moses and Elijah spoke of Christ's coming exodus in Jerusalem
Peter's eyewitness testimony of the Transfiguration, receiving honor and glory from the Father's voice
The disciples' prostration reflects the truth that no one can see God's full glory and live
Moses' face shone after encountering God on Sinai, a type of Christ's far greater radiance at the Transfiguration
The Father's command 'listen to him' fulfills the promise of the Prophet like Moses whom Israel must heed
The Word became flesh and we beheld his glory -- the Transfiguration is the supreme beholding of that glory
Christ's glorified body at the Transfiguration anticipates the transformation of believers' bodies at the resurrection
The apocalyptic vision of Christ with face shining like the sun parallels the Transfiguration radiance
